title: Multifractal and Ergodic Properties of Conductance Fluctuations under Strong Disorder
authors: Marcos A. A. de Sousa, Heitor R. Publio, Henrique A. de Lima, Adauto J. F. de Souza, Fernando A. Oliveira, Anderson L. R. Barbosa
abstract: Understanding the stochastic properties of conductance fluctuations in disordered mesoscopic systems is fundamental to quantum transport. In this work, we investigate the multifractal and ergodic properties of the fictitious time series of conductance in two-dimensional tight-binding models under varying Anderson disorder. Using standard multifractal analysis, we show that conductance fluctuations exhibit a transition from non-ergodic to ergodic behavior as the disorder strength increases, as evidenced by the decay of the conductance correlation function. Remarkably, multifractality persists in both regimes; however, it becomes insensitive to shuffling in the strong-disorder (ergodic) regime, suggesting that distributional effects dominate temporal organization. On the contrary, in the weakly disordered (non-ergodic) regime, long-range correlations play a significant role. These findings are robust against changes in lead geometry (asymmetric vs. symmetric). Our results provide new insights into the interplay between ergodicity, multifractality, and rare events in disordered quantum transport.
summary: This work analyzes how conductance fluctuations behave in disordered quantum conductors by examining their multifractal and ergodic properties. It finds a clear transition from non-ergodic to ergodic dynamics as disorder increases. Crucially, it distinguishes the physical origin of multifractality, showing it stems from temporal correlations in the weak disorder limit and from non-Gaussian probability distributions in the strong disorder limit.
main_problem: The study investigates the multifractal and ergodic properties of conductance fluctuations in disordered mesoscopic systems.
main_result: The system undergoes a transition from non-ergodic to ergodic behavior as disorder increases, with multifractality arising from temporal correlations in the non-ergodic regime and non-Gaussian PDF effects in the ergodic regime.
method: The analysis employs Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (MF-DFA) on a fictitious time series of conductance, comparing results with shuffled time series to distinguish correlation sources.
model_or_system: The model is a two-dimensional (2D) tight-binding lattice subjected to Anderson disorder via on-site potentials. Transport is analyzed through the conductance, calculated using the Landauer-Büttiker formalism.
key_observables: Conductance correlation function, generalized Hurst exponent h(q), singularity spectrum f(alpha), fictitious correlation time $\epsilon$, and non-Gaussian indicator $\eta$.
important_parameters: Disorder strength ($U$), which controls the transition between non-ergodic and ergodic regimes.
main_assumptions: The analysis treats conductance as a function of a control parameter (like Fermi energy) to generate a time series for fluctuation analysis.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the transition from non-ergodic to ergodic behavior via correlation function decay, and show how multifractal scaling parameters ($\Delta\alpha$, $h(q)$) change with disorder strength $U$ for both original and shuffled time series.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the conductance fluctuations by first establishing the physical model and observables, then applying multifractal analysis to characterize the scaling properties in different disorder regimes, culminating in a discussion of the physical origin of the observed multifractality.
why_interesting: The findings provide a detailed understanding of how fundamental concepts like ergodicity and multifractality manifest in quantum transport, offering insights into the role of rare events and non-Gaussian statistics in disordered quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Mesoscopic two-dimensional sample (in blue) con- nected to asymmetric (on the left) and symmetric (on the right) leads (in red) while being subjected to strong disorder. Both samples have dimensions of 61a × 61a, where a is the lattice constant.

FIG. 2. Panel (a) shows the conductance as a function of the Fermi energy E from a 2D sample with asymmetric leads for different values of disorder U, as illustrated in Figure 1. Panel (b) shows the conductance correlation C(δE) calculated from the conductance fictitious time series as illustrated in panel (a). Panel (c) shows the fictitious correlation time ϵ as a function of disorder U for a 2D sample connected to asymmetric (circle symbols) and symmetric (square symbols) leads.

FIG. 3. Panel (a) presents the generalized Hurst exponents, h(q), and panel (b) shows the multifractal singularity spectra, f(α), for the conductance time series of a 2D sample with asymmetric leads, as illustrated in Figure 2, at various disorder values U. Panel (c) plots the width ∆α = αmax −αmin, derived from panel (b), as a function of U for original (circle symbols) and shuffled (square symbols) time series. Panel (d) shows h(q) versus U, and panel (e) shows the Hurst exponent from shuffled time series as a function of U.

Figure 3(a) shows the Hurst exponent average h(q) as a function of q calculated from Eq. 5, for various disorder values U. At U = t, the Hurst exponent demonstrates a significant dependence on q, indicating clear multifrac- tality in the conductance time series. As the disorder in- creases, the energy correlation length decreases, leading to h(q) becoming independent of q. This trend indicates that the conductance time series tend to be monofractal. These results agree with Refs. [6, 7]. In contrast, for U > 5t, where ϵ →0, h(q) begins to change with q, sig- nifying a return to multifractality. This behavior is more evident when plotting the Hurst exponent as a function of disorder U, as...

FIG. 4. Panel (a) illustrates the conductance as a function of disorder U for 36,000 different 2D samples with asymmet- ric leads, represented by the red dotted lines. The black line depicts the average conductance across these 36,000 samples. Panel (b) presents the average conductance calculated from varying numbers of samples. Panel (c) shows the probabil- ity distribution function (PDF) of conductance for different values of U. Panel (d) displays the non-Gaussian term η, as defined in Eq. 8, as a function of U.
